title: The posture reset
goal: You can get from 'just picked it up' to 'ready to play, relaxed' in ten seconds, standing or sitting.

title: Meet your tone (ears first)
goal: You can hear the difference between plucking near the neck and near the bridge, and between tone knob up and down.

title: Match each open string to its reference
goal: Each open string rings against its reference tone with no wobble — four strings, under a minute.

title: The 5th-fret cross-check
goal: Each fretted note and the open string above it sound identical — if one pair wobbles, you know exactly which string to fix.

title: Quarters and rests, spoken and played
goal: Notes land exactly on the click, rests are exactly silent (string stopped, not fading), and your counting never pauses.

title: Eighth notes with the '&' spoken
goal: A full bar of eighths sits evenly inside the beat — no rushing the '&'s — at 80 bpm with alternating fingers.

title: The dotted-quarter + eighth (the classic)
goal: The second note lands cleanly on the '& of 2' and '& of 4' — the lopsided-on-purpose feel that powers half of pop bass.

title: Lines: see it, say it, play it
goal: You name each line note out loud a beat before you play it, in time, at 60 bpm.

title: First real reading: lines and spaces mixed
goal: Eight mixed notes read cold — no mnemonics recited, just landmarks — cleanly at 60 bpm.

title: Two-lens reading
goal: You can play the page's phrase three ways: from the stacked pair, from tab alone (recalling the rhythm), and from notation alone (choosing your own frets).

title: One phrase, two fingerings
goal: You can play D–E–G twice — once using open strings, once fully fretted at the 5th position — and hear that pitch is identical while feel differs.

title: Waltz bass: own beat 1 in 3/4
goal: Beat 1 lands like furniture being set down — solid, unhurried — while 2 and 3 stay light, for a full minute without drifting.

title: 6/8: play the two big pulses
goal: You sound only the two dotted-quarter pulses per bar while counting all six eighths out loud — the rolling feel appears by itself.

title: Root–fifth, walked up the neck
goal: The fifth shape lands without looking, from four different roots, both as up-a-fifth and down-a-fifth (same fret, string below).

title: Major vs minor third: hear the color flip
goal: From the same root, you can play both thirds back-to-back and hear (and name) happy vs. sad — from any root you pick.

title: Octave jumps in time
goal: Root–octave–root bounces cleanly (octave = 2 strings up, 2 frets over) with the octave popping out evenly, disco-style, at 90 bpm.

title: Natural notes up the E string
goal: You can play E–F–G–A–B–C–D–E up the low string, naming each note out loud, without pausing to count frets.

title: Natural notes up the A string
goal: Same drill on the A string — and you can find C (fret 3) instantly, eyes closed.

title: Octave shapes across the neck
goal: From any note on the E or A string, your hand finds the octave (two strings up, two frets over) without looking.

title: The 1-2-3-4 chromatic warmup
goal: All four fingers place notes of equal volume and length across all four strings in 3rd position — no flams, no buzzes — at 70 bpm.

title: The two-position shift
goal: The shift from 3rd to 5th position happens during the long note — silently, calmly — and the landing note speaks exactly on the beat.

title: Root motion: I–V–vi–IV in three keys
goal: Two bars per chord, roots only, in C, then G, then D — announced by numbers ('one… five… six-minor… four…') and landed without hesitation.

title: The ii–V–I with thirds
goal: Dm7–G7–Cmaj7 outlined as root+third per chord, so each chord's quality is audible from the bass alone.

title: Play–sing–check: fifths and octaves
goal: You can sing the fifth or octave above a played root before you play it — and the check note proves you right, 8 times out of 10.

title: Major and minor thirds, in time
goal: Alternating major/minor thirds from moving roots, in steady time, while calling out 'major' or 'minor' — no pausing to compute.

title: Roots around the circle
goal: You can play the circle's roots in order — C G D A E B F♯ D♭ A♭ E♭ B♭ F — two beats each, without consulting the diagram.

title: One scale shape, four keys
goal: The major-scale box lands cleanly in G, D, A, and E — proving to your hands that new sharps don't mean new fingerings.

title: One octave, degrees out loud
goal: G major up and down while saying '1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8' — then the same shape from A and from C without re-thinking the fingering.

title: Skips: 1-3, 2-4, 3-5…
goal: Playing the scale in thirds (skip one degree each time) stays clean and you can still name what you're on — the drill that turns scales into music.

title: Natural minor, one octave, degrees aloud
goal: A minor up and down, calling '1 2 ♭3 4 5 ♭6 ♭7 8' — the flats said out loud — then the same shape from E.

title: The parallel flip: A major ↔ A minor
goal: You can play both scales from the same root back-to-back and your ear tracks exactly which three notes moved (3, 6, 7 — each one fret down).

title: The box, up and down, degrees aloud
goal: A minor pentatonic box flows evenly at 90 bpm, degrees spoken ('1, ♭3, 4, 5, ♭7, 8'), from A and then E without re-thinking.

title: The ♭5 sting (pass through, don't live there)
goal: The chromatic 4–♭5–5 move slides smoothly inside a line — the ♭5 always resolving, never parked on a strong beat.

title: The four seventh arpeggios from C
goal: Cmaj7 → C7 → Cm7 → Cø7 arpeggios in sequence, one position each, naming the note that changed between neighbors.

title: Symbol flashcard sprint (no instrument)
goal: Given any of Dm7, G7, Cmaj7, Eø7, F/A, Bb7 — you can say the root, the quality, and the one note you'd play first, within two seconds each.

title: Major and minor arpeggios, one position
goal: G, then Gm, then A, then Am arpeggios (root–3rd–5th–octave–back) at 80 bpm — you always know whether your next note is the major or minor third.

title: Blind quality check (self-test)
goal: Using the demo above with eyes off the screen: 8 of 10 triad plays identified as major/minor by ear alone.
